replies.author: @jjc5407
replies.text:
To be honest it wasn't difficult.  Her lies were so blatant and easily exposed.

"Johnny smashed up the kitchen including the vase on the island."
"Um, you mean this perfectly pristine kitchen and that intact vase that we can see on the police bodycam footage after they responded to your call?"
"Oh, er, it was such an upsetting episode that we... er... cleaned up... and that's a different vase... honest."

"Johnny lost his fingertip when he smashed the kitchen phone in anger."
"Um, you mean the phone that's perfectly intact on the counter in that photo?"
"Oh, er, no.  There was another phone on the wall just out of shot... honest."
"There were two phones in this kitchen?"
"Yeah, yeah.  You just can't see the other one.  Doesn't everyone have two phones within a few feet of each other in their kitchen?"

"I was trapped in the bedroom and couldn't escape."
"Um, the bedroom with the French windows that you could have opened and walked through without Johnny even knowing?"
"Oh, er... no I was trapped I tell you!"

"He broke my nose and my ribs the night before I went on that talk show."
"The talk show where you're speaking normally and showing no discomfort whatsoever when you move?"
"I used ice on my nose, all the bruises were covered by make up and I took painkillers."

"So a bit of ice is all that's needed to fix a broken nose and prevent it from affecting your speaking voice?  And you know of painkillers that will stop broken ribs from causing excruciating pain when you move?"
"Oh, er, yeah of course.  Surely everyone knows that right?"

"I gave away the $7m Johnny paid me in the divorce.  I didn't want any of it."
"The charities you say you gave it to say they haven't received this money."
"Oh, er, well the words "give" and "pledge" are synonymous."
"No they're not."
"Oh, er, well I am planning on giving them the money.  I... er,... I just wanted to hold onto it for a while because.... um... because... well it was all these legal proceedings..."
"The legal proceedings that started sometime after you received the money?"
"Oh, er..."

replies.author: @victorcates9330
replies.text:
Justice is technically meant to be blind, but one of the only defences you have is having established your character. If you've been demonstrably good for 20 years, it's less believable if someone claims you woke up one morning and turned into the devil. I think Winona and Paradis might have issued Depp support. I think they had to word it carefully. So trying to nail Johnny to the wall seemed like you'd have to pretend that you didn't know what you know about human nature. Similarly, people were prepared to ruin lives with infantile or politically motivated noise. They rejected the notion that someone could lie. They rejected the notion that a relationship might be mutually abusive. It was splitting the world into perfect saintly victims and monsters who needed to be put into permanent exile.

And then you have to not point out how many actors had proudly or gleefully worked with Roman Polanski. Even by a standard of believing all victims (and absolutely), it was a problem how many actresses worked with Woody Allen.

For reference, I believed Amber. I didn't have a good reason for it. I have tall poppy syndrome. Depp's popularity meant that I was biased against him. So I have the moral problem of knowing that I was prepared to be amused by his downfall - which would have devastated him and presumably his young daughter. I should deal with the misery I was prepared to inflict on another human being. People were wrong about Depp (at least substantially). It seems worse if you nail someone to the wall with justifications that you don't believe for a second. Women don't believe women. They've met them. When someone says 'Believe all women', I ask the question 'What's the worst thing a woman has ever done to you?'. If women have tormented you for sport (think mean girls at high school) or your mother neglected you because she was more important than you, then asserting women have impeccable characters is something you know to be false.

Awake, but never woke. Real eyes realize real lies.

replies.author: @dawnfire82
replies.text: ​@CsykKrit I'm an Army judge advocate and that's insulting and wrong. The actual reasons are 1) back then, criminal discipline for sexual offenses was wholly in the realm of the accused Soldier's chain of command. Every Soldier she accused being in a different unit means their commanders would all be different and have no idea of the previous incidents; likely, neither did the accused Soldier so he wouldn't know to bring it up. 2) Past sexual misadventures are typically disallowed as evidence, unless **directly** relevant. A previous accusation would have to have already been proven false to be relevant. 3) Summary court martials (alluded to in the OP's description) are informal, hurried, and likely do not involve defense attorneys. 3) People change duties regularly. '6 months plus a year plus a year,' etc., means commanders would have changed. People get promoted, retire, and move on. 4) Easy Internet communications were not a thing. It was not a simple matter to look up possible witnesses or persons of interest in the GAL and make contact. So even if you were suspicious or thought someone from a previous encounter would have relevant information, there was almost no way to find them.

replies.author: @dawnfire82
replies.text: @Lanninsterlion Summary court martials don't send people to prison. They also cannot discharge personnel. They are basically an Article 15+.

replies.author: @mac_mcleod
replies.text:
@insertnamehere5809 FYI, the quote is more often credited to Socrates.    However, the quote is in the works of *neither* Aristotle or Socrates (or any other ancient philosopher) and has no primary source older than 1604/1605.  The line first appears in early-modern English sources (Elizabethan/Jacobean proverb/verse)  by Dekker.

– Page 170–171, Dekker’s 1604/1605 play The Honest Wh#re, Part I, character George speaking.

"Geo: Why I’d fain see a woman weep out her eyes, that's as true as to say, a man’s cloak burns, when it hangs in the water: I know you’ll weep, mistress, but what says the painted cloth?

Geo: Trust not a woman when she cries,
For she’ll pump water from her eyes
With a wet finger, and in faster showers,
Than April when he rains down flowers.

Vio: Ay, but George, that painted cloth is worthy to be hanged up for lying…"


Want to hear the ironic part?   I was double checking your attribution to Aristotle and found my attribution to Socrates was also wrong.  So all the quote sites assigning the quote to Socrates are wrong and feeding off of each other.   Internet rot.
